content.assessment: Delaying selection to age 14 would collapse the current geographically uneven track network into a single entry point, potentially reducing the spatial insulation effect documented for 6/8-year tracks (concentrated in cities/county seats, nearly absent in villages). This could redistribute enrollment pressure more evenly across school types precisely when overall cohort size is contracting sharply. However, the demographic contraction itself (20% birth decline in four years, regional variance exceeding 20% in several counties) means any restructuring—consolidation of entry points—will interact with an already-underway wave of closures/mergers (270 schools at risk), making disentangling reform-driven change from demographic-driven change difficult. The planning horizon for such a reform must reckon with a shrinking and geographically uneven cohort base, not a stable one.
opportunities: Consolidating entry points during a period of forced network restructuring (270 schools at risk) could reduce the marginal disruption cost relative to implementing the same reform in a stable-cohort environment
strengths: Single entry point could reduce the geographic/urban concentration advantage currently held by 6/8-year tracks
Aligns the reform timeline with a period of already-necessary network consolidation driven by demographic contraction
threats: Demographic contraction is already forcing school mergers independent of tracking reform; layering a selection-age change on top of this front-line closure wave risks compounding administrative and community disruption during an already fragile transition period
weaknesses: No cohort-specific evidence in this lens's findings on how a delayed-selection model would interact with the specific regional variance in birth decline (20%+ in several counties)
